content.problemStatement:
Ved is a salesman. He needs to earn at least WWW rupees in NNN days for his livelihood. However, on a given day iii (1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N), he can only earn AiA_iAi​ rupees by working on that day.

Ved, being a lazy salesman, wants to take as many holidays as possible. It is known that on a **holiday**, Ved does not work and thus does not earn anything. Help **maximize** the number of days on which Ved can take a holiday.

It is guaranteed that Ved can always earn at least WWW rupees by working on all days.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and WWW - the size of the array AAA and the money Ved has to earn in those NNN days.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the money which Ved can earn on each day.


Output Format
For each test case, output the maximum number of holidays Ved can take.
constraints: The sum of all Ai in a test case is guaranteed to be at least W.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and W — the number of days and the minimum total earnings required. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN, where Ai is the amount Ved can earn if he works on day i.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line — the maximum possible number of holidays Ved can take while still earning at least W rupees.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Work on days with earnings 6,3,3 (total 12 ≥ 10). 2 days are left as holidays.\nTest case 2: All four days are needed to reach 15, so 0 holidays.\nTest case 3: Working only on the day with 12 earnings already satisfies W=8, so 5 holidays.

hidden.label: edge case – exact sum of two largest

solution.approach: To minimise the number of working days (and thus maximise holidays) we should always pick the days with the largest earnings first. Sort the array A in non‑increasing order, then keep adding values from the beginning until the accumulated sum reaches or exceeds W. Let k be the number of taken days; the answer is N‑k. This greedy choice is optimal because all Ai are positive – replacing any chosen day by a smaller‑earning day can only increase the number of days needed to reach the same total.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case for sorting; the linear scan is O(N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the array (in‑place sorting can be used).

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); W = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        arr.sort(reverse=True)
        s = 0
        used = 0
        for val in arr:
            s += val
            used += 1
            if s >= W:
                break
        out_lines.append(str(N - used))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
